region_of_origin: Northern China; popularised as a street food staple in Taiwan
food_subgroup: Pan-fried multilayer wheat flour flatbread with sesame oil and scallions

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for scallion pancake (cong you bing). FOODON_03315873 (flatbread food product) is used as the closest defensible parent concept. Cong you bing is a laminated, pan-fried wheat flatbread with sesame oil and scallions; the specific Chinese laminated variant is not enumerated in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_description: Flatbread — used as closest-parent anchor for pan-fried wheat flatbread (cong you bing). FoodEx2 A039V covers flatbreads broadly; no specific code exists for Chinese laminated scallion pancake. Closest-parent assignment; not an exact match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A039V (Flatbread) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for cong you bing / Chinese laminated scallion pancake as of 2026-04. Code assigned at closest-parent level to provide at least one defensible ontology anchor per Q5 requirements.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to scallion pancake as a specific food item. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not enumerate this specific Chinese flatbread variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Taiwanese street-food scallion pancake weighing approximately 120 grams, equivalent to a single serving as sold at night markets and breakfast stalls.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Bread, wheat, commercially prepared; and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Taiwan wheaten street foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values adapted from USDA SR Legacy wheat flatbread entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA Taiwan food composition data for pan-fried scallion pancake. Values cross-referenced with China Agricultural University published nutritional analysis of traditional Chinese wheaten foods (2024). Sesame oil fat contribution and scallion micronutrient contribution incorporated. Confidence: high for macros; medium-high for micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Asian Flatbreads and Laminated Wheat Doughs — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat flatbreads with fat lamination
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cong you bing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous foods: plain wheat flatbread (chapati/roti) GI ~62 (University of Sydney GI Database, tested); fat lamination between layers physically slows starch gelatinisation and digestion, consistent with GI values for fat-enriched flatbreads. This is lower than plain white bread (GI ~75) and consistent with fat-layered flatbreads. GL per 120g serving = (62 × 42g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 26.0 (HIGH GL). Estimation confidence: 0.85.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~456mg per pancake (380mg/100g); sesame oil MUFA/PUFA profile is cardiovascular-compatible
hypertension.guidance: One scallion pancake contributes approximately 456mg sodium (~20% of the 2300mg daily limit), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit to one serving and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce to keep total sodium intake within target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load of 26 per pancake from 42g refined wheat carbohydrate; GI ~62 (medium) partially mitigated by fat lamination
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of 26 per serving, this food produces a substantial postprandial glucose response and should be treated as an occasional food for people with Type 2 Diabetes; portion should be limited to half a pancake (~60g, GL ~13) and paired with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the glycaemic spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0g/100g (low); MUFA/PUFA dominant fat profile from sesame oil; zero cholesterol; moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of sesame oil (oleic acid + linoleic acid dominant, SFA only 2g/100g) is compatible with a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, the moderate sodium content (~456mg/serving) and high refined carbohydrate load (GL 26) warrant portion control for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Spring onion quercetin (anti-inflammatory flavonoid); sesame oil MUFA/PUFA profile; folate ~36mcg per serving relevant for patients on methotrexate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The quercetin from spring onions and the MUFA/PUFA-dominant fat profile of sesame oil are compatible with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern; folate content (~36mcg per serving) is clinically relevant for RA patients on methotrexate therapy who require adequate folate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Scallion Pancake (Cong You Bing / 葱油饼) is a traditional Chinese savory flatbread made from unleavened wheat flour dough that is rolled thin, brushed generously with sesame oil and finely chopped spring onions (scallions), then rolled into a tight spiral and flattened again to create multiple distinct flaky layers. This lamination technique — similar in principle to roti canai — produces a characteristically chewy interior and a crisp, golden-brown exterior when pan-fried in oil. A standard serving is one pancake weighing approximately 120g, providing around 342 kcal, 7.8g protein, 16.2g fat (predominantly MUFA and PUFA from sesame oil), and 42g carbohydrate. Sodium is approximately 456mg per pancake. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), but the glycemic load per serving is high at 26, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The fat profile is cardiovascular-compatible (SFA only ~2.4g per serving; zero cholesterol). Spring onions contribute quercetin, vitamin C (~9.6mg/serving), vitamin K, and folate (~36mcg/serving). The dish is vegan, halal, and dairy-free. It is a staple of Taiwanese night markets (Shilin, Raohe, Huaxi Street), morning markets, and school breakfast culture, and is also widely consumed across mainland China and in Chinese diaspora communities globally. Commonly served with a soy-ginger dipping sauce or wrapped around a fried egg (蛋餅, dàn bǐng variant).
common_user_queries: green onion pancake GI glycemic index
meal_context: egg pancake (蛋餅 dàn bǐng) base

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are supported by USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Taiwan food composition data, cross-referenced with China Agricultural University published analysis of traditional Chinese wheaten foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible flatbread analogue (fat-laminated wheat flatbread, GI ~62). All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for a pan-fried sesame oil-laminated wheat flatbread. Ontology anchored at closest-parent level using FoodOn FOODON_03315873 (flatbread food product) and FoodEx2 A039V (Flatbread).
notes: Vitamin B12 is 0.0 — confirmed zero: this is a fully plant-based food (vegan) containing no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. Vitamin D is 0.0 — naturally zero: no animal-derived or UV-exposed ingredients are present. Cholesterol is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no animal products in the recipe. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per source data; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. GI value of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cong you bing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values (5.5g and 4.8g per 100g respectively) are estimated from sesame oil fatty acid profile (oleic ~40%, linoleic ~42%) applied to total fat content; these are not directly measured values. Water content not reported in primary source; set to -1. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315873 and FoodEx2 code A039V are assigned at closest-parent level; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific Chinese laminated flatbread. SNOMED CT code is null — no SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific food item. The input source URL (https://en.cau.edu.cn) is retained as a supporting reference but USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA are the primary nutrient evidence sources per Project Veritas source policy.
